name: Ignatius of Antioch
summary: Ignatius wrote on church unity, the bishop, the Eucharist, and steadfastness in martyrdom while being taken to Rome for execution.
bio: Ignatius stands near the apostolic age and gives one of the clearest early witnesses to episcopal order, sacramental worship, and the Church's visible unity. His surviving letters were written on the road to martyrdom.

works.title: Letter to the Smyrnaeans
works.summary: A warning against docetism and a strong witness to the reality of Christ's flesh and the Eucharist.

sections.title: Against Unreal Christology
sections.excerpt: Let no man deceive himself. Even the heavenly beings, and the glory of the angels, and the rulers, both visible and invisible, if they believe not in the blood of Christ, judgment awaits them also.

sections.title: The Eucharist and the Flesh of Christ
sections.excerpt: They abstain from the Eucharist and from prayer, because they confess not the Eucharist to be the flesh of our Saviour Jesus Christ, which suffered for our sins, and which the Father of His goodness raised up again.

sections.title: Follow the Bishop and the Church
sections.excerpt: See that ye all follow the bishop, even as Jesus Christ does the Father, and the presbytery as ye would the apostles. Let no man do anything connected with the Church without the bishop.

works.title: Letter to the Magnesians
works.summary: An appeal for order, humility, and harmony around the bishop and presbyters.

sections.title: Harmony in Ministry
sections.excerpt: I exhort you that ye study to do all things with a divine harmony, while your bishop presides in the place of God, and your presbyters in the place of the assembly of the apostles.

sections.title: One Prayer, One Hope
sections.excerpt: As therefore the Lord did nothing without the Father, being united to Him, neither by Himself nor by the apostles, so neither do ye anything without the bishop and presbyters.

name: Justin Martyr
summary: Justin explained Christianity to the Roman world, defended the moral life of Christians, and preserved a major early description of Christian worship.
bio: Justin's apologies connect Christian doctrine with the language of philosophy while grounding the faith in Christ, prophecy, and the public worship of the Church. His writings are especially valuable for understanding second-century liturgy.

works.title: First Apology
works.summary: A formal defense of Christian worship, morals, and doctrine addressed to Roman authorities.

sections.title: The Eucharistic Gathering
sections.excerpt: After we have thus washed him who has been convinced and has assented to our teaching, we bring him to the place where those who are called brethren are assembled, in order that we may offer hearty prayers in common.

sections.title: How Christians Understand the Eucharist
sections.excerpt: For not as common bread and common drink do we receive these; but in like manner as Jesus Christ our Saviour, having been made flesh by the Word of God, had both flesh and blood for our salvation, so likewise have we been taught that the food which is blessed by the prayer of His word is the flesh and blood of that Jesus who was made flesh.

sections.title: Sunday Worship
sections.excerpt: And on the day called Sunday, all who live in cities or in the country gather together to one place, and the memoirs of the apostles or the writings of the prophets are read, as long as time permits.

works.title: Dialogue with Trypho
works.summary: A long argument for Christ from prophecy and the continuity of salvation history.

sections.title: The New Covenant and the Law
sections.excerpt: There will be no other God, O Trypho, nor was there from eternity any other existing who arranged and made all things, than He who made and disposed all this universe.

sections.title: Christ in the Scriptures
sections.excerpt: For if you had understood what has been written by the prophets, you would not have denied that He was God, Son of the only, unbegotten, unutterable God.

name: Athanasius
summary: Athanasius defended the full deity of Christ and wrote one of the Church's classic explanations of the Incarnation.
bio: As bishop of Alexandria, Athanasius became the defining anti-Arian voice of the fourth century. His works tie salvation, worship, and Scripture together around the claim that the Son is truly God and truly man.

works.title: On the Incarnation
works.source: Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, Series II, Vol. 4
works.summary: A classic account of why the Word became flesh and how Christ restores humanity.

sections.title: Why the Word Came
sections.excerpt: For this purpose, then, the incorporeal and incorruptible and immaterial Word of God entered our world. In one sense, indeed, He was not far from it before.

sections.title: The Word Became Man for Our Renewal
sections.excerpt: He was made man that we might be made God; and He manifested Himself by a body that we might receive the idea of the unseen Father.

sections.title: The Resurrection Changes Human History
sections.excerpt: Christ, by His own power, has restored the whole nature of man. The Saviour's resurrection is the mark of victory over death.

works.title: Letter on the Council of Nicaea
works.source: Nicene and Post-Nicene Fathers, Series II, Vol. 4
works.summary: A concise explanation of why Nicene language was necessary against Arian reinterpretation.

sections.title: Why Homoousios Was Used
sections.excerpt: The bishops were compelled once again to collect the sense of the Scriptures and to speak more plainly, that the irreligious might not wrest the truth to their own impiety.

sections.title: The Son Is Not a Creature
sections.excerpt: The Son is other than things originate, alone the proper offspring of the Father's essence. For this reason He is not a creature, but very Son from the Father.
